verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Unhappy souls! stricken through with all these barbarisms as with so many lances!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I doubt whether they had so much trouble when, according to the erroneous theory of Origen, they tell from heaven to earth, and were clothed in these gross bodies, as they have now in being knocked about on all sides by these strange words and sentences: not to mention that word of ill omen which says that they are infused through the channel of the human seed.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I know that it is not usual in Christian writings to criticise mere faults of style; but I thought it well to shew by a few examples how rash it is to teach what you are ignorant of, to write what you do not know: so that, when we come to the subject-matter, we may be prepared to find the same amount of wisdom.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He sends a letter, which he calls a very strong stick, as a weapon for the Bishop of Rome; and on the very subject about which the dogs are barking at him he professes entire ignorance of the question.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If he is ignorant on the subject for which ill-reports are current against him, what need was there for him to send an Apology, which contains no defence of himself, but only a confession of his ignorance?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This course is calculated to sow a crop of suspicions, not to calm them.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He gives us three opinions about the origin of souls; and his conclusion at the end is: “I do not deny that I have read each of them, and I confess that I still am ignorant.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You would suppose him to be Arcesilaus or Carneades who declare that there is no certainty; though he surpasses even them in his cautiousness; for they were driven by the intolerable ill-will which they aroused among philosophers for taking all truth out of human life, to invent the doctrines of probability, so that by making their probable assertions they might temper their agnosticism; but he merely says that he is uncertain, and does not know which of these opinions is true.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If this was all the answer he had to make, what could have induced him to invoke so great a Pontiff as the witness of his lack of theological culture.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I presume this is the lassitude about which he tells us that he is exhausted with his thirty-years journey and cannot come to Rome.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: There are a great many things of which we are all ignorant; but we do not ask for witnesses of our ignorance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to the Father, Son and Holy Ghost, as to the nativity of our Lord and Saviour, about which Isaiah cries, “Who shall declare his generation?” he speaks boldly, and a mystery of which all past ages knew nothing he claims as quite within his knowledge: this alone he does not know, the ignorance of which causes men to stumble.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to how a virgin became the mother of God, he has full knowledge; as to how he himself was born he knows nothing.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He confesses that God is the maker of souls and bodies, whether souls existed before bodies or whether they came into being with the germs of bodies, or are sent into them when they are already formed in the womb.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In any case we recognize God as their author.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The question at issue is not whether the souls were made by God or by another, but which of the three opinions which he states is true.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Of this he professes ignorance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You may find people saying that the reason for your confession of your ignorance of the three is that you do not wish to be compelled to condemn one.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You spare Tertullian and Lactantius so as not to condemn Origen with them.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As far as I remember (though I may be mistaken) I am not aware of having read that Lactantius spoke of the soul as planted at the same time as the body.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, as you say that you have read it, please to tell me in what book it is to be found, so that you may not be thought to have calumniated him in his death as you have me in my slumber.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But even here you walk with a cautious and hesitating step.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say: “I think that, among the Latins, Tertullian or Lactantius held this opinion, perhaps also some others.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You not only are in doubt about the origin of souls, but you have only ‘thoughts’ as to the opinion which each writer holds: yet the matter is of some importance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: On the question of the soul, however, you openly proclaim your ignorance, and confess your untaught condition: as to the authors, your knowledge amounts only to ‘thinking,’ hardly to ‘presuming.’

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But as to Origen alone you are quite clear.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “This is Origen’s opinion,” you say.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, let me ask you: Is the opinion sound or not?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Your reply is, “I do not know.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then why do you send me messengers and letter-carriers, who are constantly coming, merely to teach me that you are ignorant?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: To prevent the possibility of my doubting whether your incapacity is as great as you say, and thinking it possible that you are cunningly concealing all you know, you take an oath in the presence of God that up to the present moment you hold nothing for certain and definite on this subject, and that you leave it to God to know what is true, and to any one to whom it may please Him to reveal it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Through all these ages does it seem to you that there has been no one worthy of having this revealed to him?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Neither patriarch, nor prophet, nor apostle, nor martyr?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Were not these mysteries made clear even to yourself when you dwelt amidst princes and exiles?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The Lord says in the Gospel: “Father, I have revealed thy name to men.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Did he who revealed the Father keep silence on the origin of souls?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And are you astonished if your brethren are scandalized when you swear that you know nothing of a thing which the churches of Christ profess to know?
